title: Stacks and queues - which one comes out first
author: Prodipta Acharjee
concept: A stack is last-in, first-out (LIFO) - like a pile of plates. A queue is first-in, first-out (FIFO) - like a line at a door.
explanation: Both hold things in order; they differ in which end you take from. A stack adds and removes at the same end (push, pop), so the most recent thing comes out first - think undo, or a pile of plates. A queue adds at one end and removes from the other (enqueue, dequeue), so the oldest waits its turn - think a line, or print jobs. The rule is the whole idea.

exercises.question: What does this print?
hints: append adds to the end; pop removes from the end.
'b' went in last, so it comes out first.

exercises.question: A stack removes which item first?
hints: Same end in and out - a pile of plates.

exercises.question: A queue removes which item first?
hints: Think of a line at a door.
The one that's been waiting longest.
